doc.title: Interest-bearing note — issue and pay interest
doc.event: On {issueDate}, Vera Co. borrows {face} on a {termYears}, {statedRateAnnual} note issued at face, with interest paid each year-end. Prepare the issuance entry and the first interest payment.

lines.label: = face
lines.why: Stated equals market, so the PV of the note is exactly its face — the lender hands over face.
lines.trap: The moment the stated rate is unrealistic (below market, or zero), this stops being true and imputation kicks in — that's the zero-interest scenario.

lines.label: face
lines.why: Notes Payable at face, same discipline as Bonds Payable.
lines.trap: Notes and bonds follow identical valuation rules — a note is not exempt from present-value thinking just because it's private.

lines.label: face × rate
lines.why: At face there's nothing to amortize, so expense IS the cash interest: face × stated rate × 12/12.
lines.trap: One rate, one base — the clean case. Save the two-rate machinery for notes issued off-face.

lines.label: face × rate
lines.trap: Annual, not semiannual, here — always check the payment frequency before multiplying by a fraction.

computationPaths.narration: A note issued at face is the simplest long-term liability alive: cash in at face, interest = face × stated each period, face back at maturity. Every complication in this chapter is a departure from this baseline.

memorize.body: Notes payable = bonds payable with a private lender. Same PV valuation, same amortization logic when issued off-face — don't learn two rulebooks when one covers both.
If the note's payment date isn't the fiscal year-end, the same accrual-fraction machinery from bonds applies — accrue the elapsed months.

questions.prompt: A 3-year note is issued at face with a 10% stated rate when the market rate is also 10%. What is year-1 interest expense?

distractors.feedback: This note pays ANNUALLY — no ÷2. Check the payment frequency before reflexively halving.

traces.label: Face ↔ annual coupon ↔ expense (all one number family)

build.scaffold: Rates equal → no discount, no premium, no drama. Two two-line entries: cash-for-note, then expense-for-cash each year.
